readme.text:
sugarcube

Design tokens in, CSS & components out

--- Sugarcube is a tool for building front ends on [DTCG design tokens](https://www.designtokens.org/). It connects your tokens to CSS variables, utility classes, CUBE CSS, and copy-to-own components — via the Vite plugin or CLI. ## Quick start ```bash npx @sugarcube-sh/cli init ``` This walks you through setup: starter tokens, CUBE CSS, components, and Vite plugin. The CLI is installed locally, so you can use `sugarcube` going forward. **With the Vite plugin:** ```ts // vite.config.ts import sugarcube from "@sugarcube-sh/vite"; export default { plugins: [sugarcube()], }; ``` ```ts import "virtual:sugarcube.css"; ``` **Without the Vite plugin:** ```bash sugarcube generate ``` Or add it to your npm scripts: ```json { "scripts": { "styles:generate": "sugarcube generate", "styles:generate:watch": "sugarcube generate --watch", "tokens:validate": "sugarcube validate" } } ``` ## Documentation [sugarcube.sh/docs](https://sugarcube.sh/docs) ## Packages | Package | Description | |---------|-------------| | [`@sugarcube-sh/cli`](https://npmx.dev/package/@sugarcube-sh/cli) | CLI for initialization, generation, and components | | [`@sugarcube-sh/vite`](https://npmx.dev/package/@sugarcube-sh/vite) | Vite plugin with HMR | | [`@sugarcube-sh/core`](https://npmx.dev/package/@sugarcube-sh/core) | Core token processing | ## License See [LICENSE.md](./LICENSE.md).

evidence.title: @sugarcube-sh/core@0.2.2
evidence.excerpt: ### Patch Changes - 5c23423: Fix `$root` tokens so they produce clean CSS variables. If you had a group like `blue` with a `$root` child for the base colour plus variants (`50`, `100`, etc.), the base colour was coming out as `--blue-$root` — not valid CSS. Now it simply becomes `--blue`, which is what you'd expect. References like `{blue.$root}` compile to `var(--blue)` too. ```json { "blue": { "$type": "color", "$root": { "$value": "#0000FF" }, "50": { "$value": "#ADD8E6" } } } ``` ```css /* Before */ --blue-$root: #0000ff; --blue-50: #add8e6; /* After */ --blue: #0000ff; --blue-50: #add8e6; ```

scout.summary: Sugarcube is a TypeScript-based toolchain that transforms DTCG-compliant design tokens into CSS variables, utility classes, CUBE CSS, and components via a CLI or Vite plugin. It emphasizes developer experience with features like HMR, watch mode, and token validation, and has recently fixed critical CSS variable generation issues.
highlights: Transforms design tokens into production-ready CSS and components using DTCG standards
Offers both CLI and Vite plugin with HMR for seamless integration into modern front-end workflows
Recently resolved a bug where $root tokens generated invalid CSS variable names (e.g., --blue-$root → --blue)
Actively maintained with recent patch releases and clear package modularity (@core, @cli, @vite)
risks: Open issue on DTCG-compliant Fluid Space & Type suggests incomplete support for advanced token features
Low community adoption (96 stars, 2 forks) may impact long-term maintenance and ecosystem growth

inventor.summary: Sugarcube is an inventive system that bridges DTCG design tokens to front-end artifacts—CSS variables, utility classes, CUBE CSS, and components—via a modular CLI and Vite plugin, with recent innovations fixing core token-to-CSS mapping logic.
highlights: Introduces a clean mapping from $root token syntax to valid CSS variable names (e.g., {blue.$root} → var(--blue)), solving a fundamental interoperability flaw
Architects a composable toolchain split into @core (token processing), @cli (generation/validation), and @vite (HMR integration) for flexible adoption
Enables fluid design systems by planning DTCG-compliant Fluid Space & Type support, indicating forward-looking token expressiveness
Embeds developer ergonomics via watch mode, validation scripts, and starter workflows directly in the CLI
risks: Incomplete implementation of DTCG Fluid Space & Type may limit adoption in advanced responsive design scenarios
Tight coupling between token structure (e.g., $root convention) and output assumes specific authoring patterns, potentially reducing flexibility

engineer.summary: Sugarcube is a TypeScript-based design token pipeline that reliably converts DTCG-compliant tokens into CSS variables, utility classes, and components via a modular CLI and Vite plugin, with recent fixes ensuring valid CSS output and clear developer workflows.
highlights: Core token processing correctly maps $root tokens to valid CSS variables (e.g., --blue instead of --blue-$root), resolving a critical correctness issue
Modular architecture separates concerns into @sugarcube-sh/core, @sugarcube-sh/cli, and @sugarcube-sh/vite for maintainability and targeted integration
Provides built-in scripts for generation, watch mode, and validation, enabling robust local development and CI usage
Actively patched with dependency updates and bug fixes, indicating responsive maintenance
risks: Open feature request for DTCG-compliant Fluid Space & Type suggests gaps in advanced responsive token support
Low adoption metrics (96 stars, 2 forks) may signal limited real-world testing or community-driven resilience

skeptic.summary: Sugarcube claims to bridge DTCG design tokens to CSS and components, but its practical maturity is questionable given limited adoption, unresolved advanced feature gaps, and recent fixes for basic correctness issues.
highlights: Fixed a fundamental bug where $root tokens produced invalid CSS variables (e.g., --blue-$root), suggesting initial implementation lacked basic validation
Despite modular architecture, the project shows signs of early-stage instability—critical fixes shipped in patch releases indicate incomplete initial design
Open issue on DTCG-compliant Fluid Space & Type implies core token standard support is still partial, undermining claims of DTCG compliance
risks: Low community traction (96 stars, 2 forks) raises doubts about real-world robustness and long-term viability
Reliance on custom conventions like $root may not align with broader DTCG ecosystem practices, risking interoperability
Recent patch fixes for basic CSS output suggest insufficient testing or spec adherence during initial development
